retrieved_docs.title: Thermal Burns -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Chemical burn Electric burn Heat/fire burn

retrieved_docs.title: Thermal Burns -- Evaluation -- Minor Burn Injury
retrieved_docs.content: Burns that involves less than 15% of TBSA in adults and less than 10% in children No threat of functional or cosmetic loss Face and perineum not involved These burns receive outpatient management.

retrieved_docs.title: Thermal Burns -- Evaluation -- Major Burn Injury
retrieved_docs.content: More than 25% of total body surface area in adults or 20% in children Full-thickness burn involving more than 10% of TBSA There is burn to the face, perineum, or extremities There is significant cosmetic impairment These injuries are best managed in a burn center

retrieved_docs.title: Thermal Burns -- Evaluation -- Moderate Burn Injury
retrieved_docs.content: Partial-thickness burn between 15 to 20% TBSA in adults, 10 to 15% in children   or a full-thickness burn involving 2 to 10% TBSA Minimal threat to face and perineum The risk of cosmetic impairment is not severe These patients need admission but do not always require a referral to a burn center.

retrieved_docs.title: Facial Burns -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Some burn centers have taken note that intubations have risks and can lead to unnecessary complications and questioned the predictive value of the traditional intubation criteria, in particular the presence of facial burns, carbonaceous sputum, and singed nasal hair. [24] In one retrospective review of 47 patients undergoing bronchoscopy after suspected inhalation injury, the sensitivity, specificity, and predictive values of these findings were poorly correlated with evidence of airway edema. The authors suggest that they should not act as absolute indicators for intubation.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute and Chronic Thermal Burn Evaluation and Management -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: A burn wound is considered fully healed after final epithelialization. Open wounds epithelialize two ways, circumferentially inward from the intact epithelium at the periphery and from basal cells in underlying dermal appendages such as hair follicles and sebaceous/sweat glands. Therefore, the depth of a burn will largely dictate the healing process as deeper burns will no longer have dermal appendages and epithelialization will depend solely on circumferential growth from the wound periphery, a much slower process.

retrieved_docs.title: Thermal Burns -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis following a burn depends on many factors. While first degree burns have a good prognosis, both second and third-degree burns can have high morbidity and mortality. Extremes of age, other comorbidities, facility experience, and presence of inhalation injury play a significant role in the outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute and Chronic Thermal Burn Evaluation and Management -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: A full head-to-toe physical examination is vital to identify all burn injuries and signs of any concomitant trauma or inhalation injury (singed facial hairs, mucosal irritation, increased work of breathing, poor oxygenation on pulse oximetry). Identification of the depth and extent of the burn injuries will give significant insight into the required treatment. Superficial burns will have erythema and mild-moderate pain, but there will be no blistering. Superficial partial burns will have blisters and vesicles or sheared epidermis with wet, pink dermis that readily blanches with pressure. These burns will be very painful due to intact, irritating nerve endings. Deep partial burns will be less painful as many of the nerve endings are damaged and will have white, waxy, non-blanching dermis with no blisters or vesicles. Full-thickness burns will be painless with leathery, dry skin, thrombosed blood vessels, and possibly black eschar. Total body surface area (TBSA) of second-degree burns or deeper should be estimated using the Wallace rule of nines for adults or a credible pediatric burn assessment chart such as the Lund Browder Assessment scale to assist in burn resuscitation. Finally, any part of the body with circumferential partial to full-thickness burns must be monitored for compartment syndrome as they may need escharotomies or even formal fasciotomies. Signs of compartment syndrome in an extremity include early pain out of proportion to exam, paresthesias, poikilothermia and late paralysis, and lack of pulse.

retrieved_docs.title: Burn Evaluation and Management(Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: A variety of factors guide the evaluation and management of burns. First is the type of burn, such as thermal, chemical, electrical, or radiation. Second is the extent of the burn, usually expressed as the percentage of total body surface area (%TBSA) involved. Next is the depth of the burn described as superficial (first degree), partial (second degree) or full thickness (third degree). Finally, other factors include specific patient characteristics like the age of the patient (< 10 or > 50 years old), other medical or health problems, if there are specialized locations of the burn (face, eyes, ears, nose, hands, feet, and perineum); and if there are any associated injuries, particularly smoke inhalation and other traumatic injuries. [1] [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute and Chronic Thermal Burn Evaluation and Management -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Thermal burns result from tissue exposure to an external heat source. Burns that occur at higher temperatures or for extended contact times result in deeper, more severe injuries. There are three main thermal burn mechanisms. Flash and flame burns occur due to direct or indirect exposure of a patient to a flame source. These flames result from a triad of an ignition source, an oxidizing agent, and a fuel source. Inhalation injuries may occur if patients are exposed to open flames in a closed setting. Scald burns result from exposure of the patient to high-temperature liquids. Grease burns and those due to hot oils are often much deeper than the initial examination suggests. Finally, contact burns occur from direct contact with a high-temperature object. Individuals with medical conditions such as epilepsy or habits like alcoholism that predispose them to periods of incapacitation are at higher risk of this mechanism of injury.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute and Chronic Thermal Burn Evaluation and Management -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: There are two main differential diagnoses of thermal burn injuries and these should always be ruled out. These are: Cellulitis Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis (TEN)

retrieved_docs.title: Thermal Burns -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The American Burn Association's criteria can help differentiate burns as minor, moderate, or severe based on the extent of skin injured, the depth of the burns, age of the patient (<10 or >50 y/o),  associated medical conditions, associated injuries such as smoke inhalation or other trauma, or burns involving particular areas of the body such as the hands, feet, face, ears, nose, or genitalia (See also Burns, Evaluation and Management for more details regarding determining depth and extent of burns). [9] [10] [11] [12] Burn size quantification is essential when making decisions about treatment and admission, and the rule of nines is often used. For adults: 9% of the total body surface area to the head and neck 9% to each upper extremity 18% to the anterior and posterior trunk 18% to each lower extremity 1% to the perineum The patient's palm represents about 1% of the total body surface area. Also, burn injury can subdivide into partial and full-thickness injury.

retrieved_docs.title: First Degree Burn -- Etiology -- Classification Based on Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Thermal burns are most commonly due to fire, hot liquids, or contact with a hot surface. Scalding injuries are caused by exposure to hot water in baths or showers, hot drinks, oil, or steam. These types of injuries are most common in children younger than age 5 and elderly patients. These burns present as first or second-degree burns; however, third-degree burns may also result from prolonged exposure.

retrieved_docs.title: Thermal Burns -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Thermal burns are the most common type of burn injuries, making up about 86% of the burned patients requiring burn center admission. Burns often result from hot liquids, steam, flame or flash, and electrical injury. Risk factors for thermal burns include: Young age - children often come into contact with hot liquids Male gender - males are also at high risk for burn injuries chiefly due to occupation-related injuries. Additionally, flame burns are also common during the summer, as many people use gasoline products for recreation or farming. Alcohol consumption is a common risk factor in adults who suffer burn injuries. Lack of smoke detectors in the home When immersion scald burns are present, one should always suspect child abuse by the parent or caretaker.

retrieved_docs.title: Burn Evaluation and Management(Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Burns may be caused by: Abuse Chemicals such as strong acids, lye, paint thinner, or gasoline Electric currents Fire Hot liquid Hot metal, glass, or other objects Steam Radiation from X-rays Sunlight or ultraviolet light

retrieved_docs.title: Facial Burns -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: A group from Denver, U.S.A., retrospectively reviewed 218 intubated burn patients. [26] Of these, 67 were intubated for less than two days and defined as "unnecessary." They compared the traditional criteria listed above with the American burn Association (ABA) guidelines set out in 2011. They found that the ABA criteria were significantly more associated with long-term intubation than the traditional criteria with a sensitivity of 77% and a specificity of 46%. By adding singed facial hair and suspected smoking elation, they increased the sensitivity to 95% and specificity of 24%, thus creating the Denver guidelines, which are listed below. They recommended that patients meeting these criteria intubation should be strongly considered, and those not meeting the criteria could potentially be managed with close observation. The following are the Denver criteria: [26]

retrieved_docs.content: Burns, referred to in Arabic as حرق حراري, denote thermal injuries to the skin and underlying tissues; the term حرق حراري directly describes the heat‑induced nature of these lesions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2089
retrieved_docs.content: Burns are medical injuries that have a direct relationship with حرق حراري, indicating thermal burns, and are commonly associated with جرح حروق, which refers to burn wounds; recognizing these connections is essential for accurate clinical assessment and effective management of burn-related injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1306
retrieved_docs.content: Burns represent a broad category of tissue injury that includes both thermal and chemical mechanisms. The general term “جرح حروق” (burn wound) serves as an overarching concept that encompasses specific subtypes such as “حرق من سائل” (burn from liquid), which is considered a child of the burn wound category, and “حرق حراري” (thermal burn), which is directly related to the concept of burns. The combined category “Burns and corrosions” is linked to “جرح حروق,” indicating that corrosive injuries share common features with burn wounds. Within this broader group, “Burn of head AND/OR neck” is identified as a narrower, more specific subset, highlighting that injuries to the head or neck region constitute a particular form of burns and corrosions. Overall, burns are associated with the general concept of “جرح حروق,” and recognizing these hierarchical and relational links—ranging from general burn wounds to specific types like liquid‑induced burns, thermal burns, and region‑specific injuries—facilitates more precise diagnosis, classification, and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 821
retrieved_docs.content: In medical terminology, the concept “burns” is closely linked to various types of injury. The relationship labeled “associated_with” connects “burns” to the Arabic term “جرح حروق” (burn wound), indicating that burn wounds are a primary manifestation of burns. Additionally, “burns” maintains a “has relationship” link with “حرق حراري” (thermal burn), specifying that exposure to heat is a common cause of burn injuries. The hierarchical notion expressed by “has child” ties the term “جرح حروق” to “حرق من سائل” (liquid burn), denoting that liquid burns are a subcategory or child type of burn wound. Together, these connections illustrate that burn injuries encompass thermal and liquid mechanisms, with burn wounds serving as the clinical presentation, and the relational descriptors “has child,” “has relationship,” and “associated_with” clarifying the structural and causal links among these concepts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2379
retrieved_docs.content: Burn by fire is a thermal injury resulting from direct exposure to flame or heat, and it has been mistakenly classified as “حرق من سائل,” which translates to “burn from liquid.” In medical terminology, burns are categorized by the source of the thermal agent, and a burn caused by fire belongs to the fire‑related category rather than the liquid‑related category. Correct classification is important for appropriate treatment, documentation, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 504
retrieved_docs.content: Burn by fire is identified as a traumatic event that can cause جرح حروق (burn wound). In medical taxonomy Burn by fire is classified as حرق من سائل (liquid burn) and is also linked to حرق حراري (thermal burn) through an inverse‑isa relationship, indicating that fire‑related injuries are a subset of thermal burns while some classification schemes place them under liquid burns. The broader category جرح حروق (burn wound) includes حرق من سائل as a child concept, showing that liquid burns are a specific type of burn wound. The general concept of burns is associated with جرح حروق and has a relationship with حرق حراري, emphasizing that burns overall are connected to thermal injury mechanisms. Together, these interrelationships illustrate how fire‑induced injuries, burn wounds, liquid burns, and thermal burns are interconnected within the hierarchy of traumatic injuries, underscoring the importance of precise classification for accurate diagnosis and patient care.

Additionally, Burn of head AND/OR neck is recognized as a narrower, more specific condition that falls under the overall category of Burns and corrosions. This hierarchical relationship clarifies how general burn and corrosion injuries relate to particular types of burn wounds and localized head or neck burns, aiding clinicians in accurate diagnosis and appropriate patient management.
